Content_Sketch: Pentagon office — you sit behind the desk that commands the most powerful military on Earth
Illustration_Note: SUBJECT seated at massive desk inside five-sided building, American flag behind

Content_Sketch: You never commanded more than a platoon. Now you command all of it.
Illustration_Note: Split: small platoon (30 stickmen) vs massive military formation (thousands)

Content_Sketch: Weight line: that's what loyalty costs
Illustration_Note: SUBJECT alone, dog tags visible, weight of the room pressing down

Content_Sketch: Born in a small lakeside town in Minnesota — father coaches basketball, mother runs leadership programs
Illustration_Note: Small-town main street, SUBJECT as child with COACH father holding basketball

Content_Sketch: Church on Sundays, football in fall, Norwegian heritage — small-town American normalcy
Illustration_Note: Church steeple, football field, autumn leaves, SUBJECT in varsity jacket

Content_Sketch: Class valedictorian — accepted to the Ivy League school in New Jersey
Illustration_Note: Graduation cap, valedictorian podium, then Ivy campus gates

Content_Sketch: You run the conservative student paper on a liberal campus — you start believing you see what others don't
Illustration_Note: SUBJECT at desk with newspaper, surrounded by campus protesters, isolated

Content_Sketch: ROTC — you wear the uniform across a civilian campus. People stare.
Illustration_Note: Split: SUBJECT in ROTC uniform walking through campus of casual students

Content_Sketch: First deployment: the offshore military prison — guarding detainees in Caribbean heat
Illustration_Note: Chain-link fences, watchtower, SUBJECT in combat gear, tropical heat haze

Content_Sketch: Iraq: Baghdad and Samarra — convoys, checkpoints, firefights. Two Bronze Stars.
Illustration_Note: Desert convoy, dust clouds, SUBJECT with rifle, Bronze Star ribbon overlay

Content_Sketch: Afghanistan: you teach counterinsurgency. You know the war. You've lived the war.
Illustration_Note: Training room in Kabul, SUBJECT pointing at map, soldiers listening

Content_Sketch: Home — you run for the Senate in your home state. You lose.
Illustration_Note: Campaign podium, empty chairs, election night screen showing loss

Content_Sketch: Pivot to cable television — the network offers you a microphone instead of a seat
Illustration_Note: TV studio door opening, SUBJECT stepping from desert camo into suit

Content_Sketch: Seven years on the weekend morning show — millions watch you over breakfast every Saturday
Illustration_Note: Studio couch, cameras, coffee mugs, SUBJECT with flag pin, red/blue lighting

Content_Sketch: Three marriages — the timelines overlap. Nobody says it out loud.
Illustration_Note: Three wedding rings overlapping on a timeline bar, SUBJECT looking away

Content_Sketch: You write books about warriors and decline. You believe every word.
Illustration_Note: SUBJECT at book signing, stacks of books, American flag backdrop

Content_Sketch: The president calls. 'I want you for the job.'
Illustration_Note: Phone ringing, presidential seal, SUBJECT answering

Content_Sketch: The military is shocked. The media splits. The generals say nothing.
Illustration_Note: Split screen: generals with blank faces, TV screens with opposing headlines

Content_Sketch: You've never commanded more than thirty people. They're asking you to lead three million.
Illustration_Note: Number 30 transforming into 3,000,000 — SUBJECT standing between them

Content_Sketch: Confirmation hearings — everything is pulled apart. Misconduct allegations. Drinking. Finances. Marriages.
Illustration_Note: Senate hearing room, microphones, cameras, SUBJECT at table alone

Content_Sketch: Personal messages leaked. Private life weaponized. Three marriages on every front page.
Illustration_Note: Newspaper headlines swirling, SUBJECT sitting still amid chaos

Content_Sketch: The vote: fifty to fifty. The vice president walks in. Breaks the tie.
Illustration_Note: Senate chamber, vote tally 50-50, VP figure entering, gavel falling

Content_Sketch: One person. One vote. You squeeze through the narrowest door in confirmation history.
Illustration_Note: Number 51-50 on screen, SUBJECT walking through narrow doorway

Content_Sketch: Sworn in the next day. You haven't exhaled yet.
Illustration_Note: Oath ceremony, hand on Bible, flashbulbs

Content_Sketch: Day one at the building — endless corridors. Generals line up. They salute the chair, not you.
Illustration_Note: Pentagon hallway, GENERALS standing in line, SUBJECT walking past

Content_Sketch: Week one: you fire the Army chief of staff. Reform or revenge — depends who's telling it.
Illustration_Note: Office confrontation, GENERAL being dismissed, door closing

Content_Sketch: Month one: full restructuring. Loyalists in, old guard out. Someone inside the building is leaking.
Illustration_Note: Organizational chart being redrawn, shadowy figures in corridors

Content_Sketch: You share operational details on an unsecure messaging app. A journalist is accidentally added to the chat.
Illustration_Note: Phone screen with chat bubbles, classified stamp, journalist icon appearing

Content_Sketch: The inspector general investigates. Confirms the violation. You refuse to be interviewed.
Illustration_Note: IG report document, SUBJECT turning away from interview room

Content_Sketch: The Caribbean incident — an order to shoot beyond the rules of engagement. Accusations of 'kill them all.'
Illustration_Note: Boat on dark water, military vessel, flash of gunfire

Content_Sketch: Every week a new crisis. But the president doesn't fire you. That's the only answer that matters.
Illustration_Note: SUBJECT standing alone in Pentagon office, presidential portrait on wall

Content_Sketch: September 2025 — you sign the order. Department of Defense becomes Department of War.
Illustration_Note: Pen signing document, 'Department of War' nameplate replacing old one

Content_Sketch: February 28, 2026 — joint strikes with the Middle Eastern ally. Largest military operation since 2003.
Illustration_Note: War room, screens showing strike maps, SUBJECT at head of table

Content_Sketch: Missile infrastructure, air defenses, nuclear sites — all targeted. The enemy navy nearly erased.
Illustration_Note: Explosion graphics on screens, naval vessels sinking on display

Content_Sketch: The strait closes. Oil prices spike. Retaliatory missiles and drones hit regional bases.
Illustration_Note: Strait of Hormuz map, oil price ticker climbing, drone swarms

Content_Sketch: You brief every morning — the voice that once hosted weekend TV now reads casualty reports.
Illustration_Note: SUBJECT at podium, briefing papers, cameras, ghost of TV studio behind

Content_Sketch: You fire more generals mid-war. A dozen senior officers removed, blocked, or forced out.
Illustration_Note: Stars being removed from uniforms, empty chairs at war table

Content_Sketch: Week six. The war has no end date. You don't set one.
Illustration_Note: Calendar with no end marked, SUBJECT staring at map

Content_Sketch: The press asks: 'When does it end?' You answer like someone who has never doubted himself.
Illustration_Note: Press conference, microphones, SUBJECT speaking with absolute certainty

Content_Sketch: Dog tags still cold against your chest. Iraq to Iran — twenty years. Still war.
Illustration_Note: Close on dog tags, desert dust fading to Pentagon marble

Content_Sketch: Back to the platoon image — thirty people once. Three million now. The gap between those numbers.
Illustration_Note: Ghost platoon of 30 stickmen behind SUBJECT, fading into massive military formation

Content_Sketch: That distance — that's where everything lives. And you're still standing in it.
Illustration_Note: SUBJECT alone in five-sided building, dog tags glinting, open ending
